section.title: Parables, the Storm, and Jairus
section.sectionRationale: This movement gathers Jesus' teaching about the word and a chain of mighty acts that show His authority. He preaches the kingdom with women supporting Him, tells the parable of the sower and explains it, adds the lamp and His true family, then displays His power over nature, demons, disease, and death: calming the storm, freeing the Gerasene demoniac, healing the bleeding woman, and raising Jairus' daughter. The word planted in the heart and the Lord who commands even wind and death belong together.
section.thesis: The word of God is the seed that saves those who hear it with a good heart and hold on to it, and the One who sows that word is the Lord whose bare command stills the storm, casts out Legion, heals the incurable, and raises the dead, so that faith in Him is met by His saving power.
section.frameNote: Six movements: the women who supported Jesus; the parable of the sower and its meaning; the lamp and Jesus' true family; the calming of the storm; the deliverance of the Gerasene demoniac; and the healing of the bleeding woman with the raising of Jairus' daughter.

units.label: The women who followed
units.note: supporting the mission out of their own means

verses.text: Soon afterward, Jesus traveled from one town and village to another, preaching and proclaiming the good news of the {{basileia|kingdom}} of God. The Twelve were with Him,
as well as some women who had been healed of evil spirits and infirmities: Mary called Magdalene, from whom seven demons had gone out,
Joanna the wife of Herod’s household manager Chuza, Susanna, and many others. These women were ministering to them out of their own means.

units.label: The parable of the sower
units.note: the seed is the word of God

verses.text: While a large crowd was gathering and people were coming to Jesus from town after town, He told them this parable:
“A farmer went out to sow his seed. And as he was sowing, some seed fell along the path, where it was trampled, and the birds of the air devoured it.
Some fell on rocky ground, and when it came up, the seedlings withered because they had no moisture.
Other seed fell among thorns, which grew up with it and choked the seedlings.
Still other seed fell on good soil, where it sprang up and produced a crop, a hundredfold.” As Jesus said this, He called out, “He who has ears to hear, let him hear.”
Then His disciples asked Him what this parable meant.

He replied, “The knowledge of the mysteries of the kingdom of God has been given to you, but to others I speak in parables, so that, ‘though seeing, they may not see; though hearing, they may not understand.’
Now this is the meaning of the parable: The seed is the {{logos|word}} of God.
The seeds along the path are those who hear, but the devil comes and takes away the word from their hearts, so that they may not believe and be saved.
The seeds on rocky ground are those who hear the word and receive it with joy, but they have no root. They believe for a season, but in the time of testing, they fall away.
The seeds that fell among the thorns are those who hear, but as they go on their way, they are choked by the worries, riches, and pleasures of this life, and their fruit does not mature.
But the seeds on good soil are those with a noble and good heart, who hear the word, cling to it, and by persevering produce a crop.

units.label: The lamp and Jesus' true family
units.note: hearing the word of God and carrying it out

verses.text: No one lights a lamp and covers it with a jar or puts it under a bed. Instead, he sets it on a stand, so those who enter can see the light.
For there is nothing hidden that will not be disclosed, and nothing concealed that will not be made known and brought to light.
Pay attention, therefore, to how you listen. Whoever has will be given more, but whoever does not have, even what he thinks he has will be taken away from him.”
Then Jesus’ mother and brothers came to see Him, but they were unable to reach Him because of the crowd.
He was told, “Your mother and brothers are standing outside, wanting to see You.”
But He replied, “My mother and brothers are those who hear the word of God and carry it out.”

units.label: The calming of the storm
units.note: who is this? even wind and water obey Him

verses.text: One day Jesus said to His disciples, “Let us cross to the other side of the lake.” So He got into a boat with them and set out.
As they sailed, He fell asleep, and a windstorm came down on the lake, so that the boat was being swamped, and they were in great danger.
The disciples went and woke Him, saying, “Master, Master, we are perishing!” Then Jesus got up and rebuked the wind and the raging waters, and they subsided, and all was calm.
“Where is your {{pistis|faith}}?” He asked. Frightened and amazed, they asked one another, “Who is this? He commands even the winds and the water, and they obey Him!”

units.label: The Gerasene demoniac
units.note: Legion cast out, a man restored

verses.text: Then they sailed to the region of the Gerasenes, across the lake from Galilee.
When Jesus stepped ashore, He was met by a demon-possessed man from the town. For a long time this man had not worn clothing or lived in a house, but he stayed in the tombs.
When the man saw Jesus, he cried out and fell down before Him, shouting in a loud voice, “What do You want with me, Jesus, Son of the Most High God? I beg You not to torture me!”

For Jesus had commanded the unclean spirit to come out of the man. Many times it had seized him, and though he was bound with chains and shackles, he had broken the chains and been driven by the demon into solitary places.
“What is your name?” Jesus asked. “Legion,” he replied, because many demons had gone into him.
And the demons kept begging Jesus not to order them to go into the Abyss.
There on the hillside a large herd of pigs was feeding. So the demons begged Jesus to let them enter the pigs, and He gave them permission.
Then the demons came out of the man and went into the pigs, and the herd rushed down the steep bank into the lake and was drowned.
When those tending the pigs saw what had happened, they ran off and reported this in the town and countryside.
So the people went out to see what had happened. They came to Jesus and found the man whom the demons had left, sitting at Jesus’ feet, clothed and in his right mind; and they were afraid.
Meanwhile, those who had seen it reported how the demon-possessed man had been healed.
Then all the people of the region of the Gerasenes asked Jesus to depart from them, because great fear had taken hold of them. So He got into the boat and started back.
The man whom the demons had left begged to go with Jesus. But He sent him away, saying,
“Return home and describe how much God has done for you.” So the man went away and proclaimed all over the town how much Jesus had done for him.

units.label: The bleeding woman and Jairus' daughter
units.note: do not be afraid, only believe

verses.text: When Jesus returned, the crowd welcomed Him, for they had all been waiting for Him.
Just then a synagogue leader named Jairus came and fell at Jesus’ feet. He begged Him to come to his house,
because his only daughter, who was about twelve, was dying. As Jesus went with him, the crowds pressed around Him,
including a woman who had suffered from bleeding for twelve years. She had spent all her money on physicians, but no one was able to heal her.
She came up behind Jesus and touched the fringe of His cloak, and immediately her bleeding stopped.
“Who touched Me?” Jesus asked. But they all denied it. “Master,” said Peter, “the people are crowding and pressing against You.”
But Jesus declared, “Someone touched Me, for I know that power has gone out from Me.”

Then the woman, seeing that she could not escape notice, came trembling and fell down before Him. In the presence of all the people, she explained why she had touched Him and how she had immediately been healed.
“Daughter,” said Jesus, “your faith has {{sozo|healed}} you. Go in peace.”
While He was still speaking, someone arrived from the house of the synagogue leader. “Your daughter is dead,” he told Jairus. “Do not bother the Teacher anymore.”
But Jesus overheard them and said to Jairus, “Do not be afraid; just believe, and she will be healed.”
When He entered the house, He did not allow anyone to go in with Him except Peter, John, James, and the child’s father and mother.
Meanwhile, everyone was weeping and mourning for her. But Jesus said, “Stop weeping; she is not dead but asleep.”
And they laughed at Him, knowing that she was dead.
But Jesus took her by the hand and called out, “Child, get up!”
Her spirit returned, and at once she got up. And He directed that she be given something to eat.
Her parents were astounded, but Jesus ordered them not to tell anyone what had happened.

basileia.gloss: Kingdom, reign, the active rule of God breaking into the world. It is the good news Jesus proclaims from town to town and the mystery whose secrets are given to His disciples. The kingdom advances not by force but by the sown word taking root in receptive hearts, and by the King's authority over storm, demon, disease, and death.

logos.gloss: Word, message, the spoken revelation of God. In the parable the seed is the logos of God, the very message Jesus preaches. Its fruitfulness depends not on the seed, which is good, but on the soil of the heart that receives it; the good heart hears the word, clings to it, and by perseverance bears a crop.

pistis.gloss: Faith, trust that takes God at His word. In the storm Jesus asks, 'Where is your faith?', exposing how quickly fear crowds it out even in those who follow Him. Across this movement faith is the empty hand that reaches to Jesus, whether a woman touching His cloak or a father told simply to believe.

sozo.gloss: To save, heal, rescue, make whole. It spans the healing of the body and the salvation of the whole person. Jesus tells the bleeding woman, 'Your faith has saved you,' rendered here 'healed you,' and uses the same word of Jairus' daughter, 'she will be saved.' The wholeness Jesus gives reaches beyond the body to the person.

basic.context: Jesus travels preaching the kingdom, supported by women He had healed. He tells the parable of the sower: the same word falls on four kinds of heart, and only the good heart hears, holds on, and bears fruit. He adds that a lamp is meant to give light and that His true family are those who hear God's word and do it. Then Luke stacks four demonstrations of His authority. He calms a deadly storm with a word, delivers a man ruled by a legion of demons, heals a woman who had bled for twelve years the instant she touches His cloak, and raises Jairus' twelve-year-old daughter from death. The word must take root, and the One who sows it is Lord over everything that threatens us.
basic.hermeneutics: The sower is about the hearers, not the seed: the word is always good, but hearts differ, and only perseverance proves the good soil. The four miracles are arranged to answer the disciples' question in the boat, 'Who is this?' Nature, demons, chronic disease, and death all yield to Him. Faith runs through the section, from the disciples who lose it in the storm to the woman and the father who cast themselves on Jesus.
basic.originalLanguages: Jesus proclaims the basileia, the kingdom, whose secrets are given to His disciples and whose growth depends on the sown logos, the word of God, taking root in a good heart. In the storm He asks, 'Where is your pistis, your faith?' And to the bleeding woman He says her faith has sozo, saved and healed her, the same word He uses of Jairus' daughter.
basic.typology: Jesus stilling the sea recalls the God of the Psalms who rules the raging waters and rescues the perishing. Raising Jairus' daughter with 'Child, get up' anticipates His own resurrection and the final raising of all who belong to Him. The word as seed picks up Isaiah's promise that God's word will not return empty but accomplish His purpose.
basic.christ: The disciples' cry, 'Who is this? Even wind and water obey Him,' is the question the whole movement answers. Jesus does what only God can do: He commands the sea, masters a legion of demons, heals the incurable, and calls the dead back to life. He is the sower of the word and the Lord of the kingdom, and faith in Him is never disappointed.
basic.god: God's rule is breaking in through Jesus, and it comes by His word taking root in humble hearts. He is the God whose power over chaos, evil, sickness, and death is total, and who nonetheless stoops to a frightened woman and a grieving father. What He asks in return is faith: do not be afraid, only believe.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 55 promises that God's word will not return empty. Psalm 107 pictures the Lord who stills the storm and delivers the perishing. Isaiah 6 stands behind the parable's warning about seeing yet not seeing. And Luke 24 shows the resurrection to which the raising of Jairus' daughter points.
basic.oneStory: The kingdom advances as the word is sown and takes root, and the King reveals Himself as Lord over storm, demons, disease, and death. Scripture had promised a God whose word accomplishes His will and whose power rescues the perishing. In Jesus that God has drawn near, sowing the word and answering faith with saving power, all the way to raising the dead.

context.literary: The movement pairs a block of teaching about hearing the word with a cluster of four miracles that reveal Jesus' authority. Luke frames it so that the parable's call to 'hear' and the true family's mark of doing the word set up the miracles, in which the response of faith is repeatedly tested and rewarded. The disciples' question in the boat, 'Who is this?', is the hinge the whole section turns on.
context.historical: Women funding a rabbi's ministry was notable in that culture, and Luke names them deliberately. The Sea of Galilee is prone to sudden violent squalls funneled through the surrounding hills. The Gerasene region was largely Gentile, which explains the herd of pigs. A woman with a chronic flow of blood was ceremonially unclean and socially isolated, and a synagogue ruler like Jairus risked his standing by falling at Jesus' feet.
context.canonical: The parable of the sower is foundational teaching on how people respond to the gospel, echoed wherever the New Testament speaks of hearing and enduring. The stilling of the storm places Jesus in the role Scripture gives to God alone over the sea. And the raising of Jairus' daughter belongs to the chain of resurrections that anticipates and depends on Christ's own, the firstfruits of those who sleep.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To teach that the fruitfulness of the word depends on how it is heard and held, and to reveal, through four decisive miracles, that Jesus is the Lord whose authority reaches over nature, demons, disease, and death, so that readers will hear the word with a good heart and trust Him.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The parable's teaching about the four soils is prescriptive: it calls every hearer to be good soil that perseveres. The miracles describe what Jesus did and reveal who He is; they are not a formula guaranteeing that every storm will be stilled or every sick person healed on demand, but they ground the call to faith in His proven power.

debates.question: Do the rocky and thorny soils describe people who were truly saved and then lost?

views.label: They were never truly rooted
views.case: Jesus says the rocky-ground hearers 'believe for a season' but have no root and fall away in testing, and only the good soil perseveres and bears fruit; genuine, saving reception is shown by lasting fruit, so the others had a temporary, rootless response.

views.label: A warning about real dangers to real hearers
views.case: Others stress the pastoral force: the soils warn every hearer that the devil, shallow enthusiasm, and the choking cares of life can rob the word of fruit, calling us to guard our hearing rather than to settle debates about who was 'really' saved.

debates.bearing: Either way the point stands: true hearing endures and bears fruit, so we are to receive the word with a good heart, cling to it, and persevere through testing and the cares of life.

debates.question: Why did Jesus insist on knowing who touched Him when the crowd was pressing all around?

views.label: To bring the woman's faith into the open
views.case: The healing was real the moment she touched Him, but Jesus draws her out so her secret, trembling faith becomes a public confession, and He can call her 'Daughter' and send her away in peace rather than leave her hiding in the crowd.

views.label: To distinguish faith from mere contact
views.case: Many were pressing against Jesus, but only one touched Him in faith; His question marks the difference between crowding near Him and actually trusting Him, honoring her faith before all.

debates.bearing: Both show that Jesus is not content with anonymous, magical contact; He seeks personal, confessed faith and gives the healed woman assurance and peace.

study.originalLanguages: Jesus goes from town to town proclaiming the basileia, the reign of God breaking in, and tells His disciples that its mysteries are given to them. Its growth depends on the sown logos, the word of God, which is always good seed; the harvest turns on the soil of the heart that hears it, clings to it, and by perseverance bears fruit. Then the King displays His authority. In the storm He rebukes the wind and asks, 'Where is your pistis?', for fear had swallowed their faith. And when a desperate woman touches His cloak, He tells her that her faith has sozo, saved and healed her, using the same word when He assures Jairus that his dead daughter will be saved. Faith reaches out, and the Lord's saving power answers.

typology.fulfillment: He does what Scripture ascribes to God alone, who rules the raging sea and stills its waves, Psalm 107:28-29 and Psalm 89:9.
The sown word fulfills Isaiah 55:10-11, God's word that does not return empty but accomplishes His purpose and bears fruit.
His command 'Child, get up' anticipates His own resurrection and the final raising of His people, 1 Corinthians 15:20-23.

study.christ: The whole movement answers the disciples' awestruck question, 'Who is this?' Jesus rebukes a killer storm and the sea falls silent, an act Scripture reserves for God. He masters a legion of demons that no chains could hold and restores a man to his right mind. He heals a woman whom twelve years and every physician could not, the instant her faith reaches His cloak. And He takes a dead girl by the hand and calls her back to life as easily as waking a child from sleep. He is the sower of the saving word and the Lord of the kingdom, and every kind of power that threatens us bows to His. Faith that casts itself on Him is never turned away.
study.god: God's kingdom is breaking into the world through Jesus, and it comes not by force but by the word sown in humble, persevering hearts. The God revealed here is sovereign over the sea, over the powers of darkness, over incurable disease, and over death itself, yet He bends low to a trembling outcast woman and a frightened father. To both His word is the same: do not be afraid, only believe. He answers faith with saving power and gives peace to those who come to Him.

crossReferences.note: God's word, like seed and rain, does not return empty but accomplishes His purpose, the truth behind the parable of the sower.
The Lord stills the storm and brings the perishing to their haven, the role Jesus fills in calming the sea.
Seeing yet not perceiving, quoted by Jesus to explain why He teaches the crowds in parables.
Christ the firstfruits of the raised, the resurrection to which the raising of Jairus' daughter points.

study.oneStory: The kingdom of God advances as the word is sown and takes root in good hearts, and the King who sows it proves to be Lord over storm, demons, disease, and death. Scripture had promised a God whose word accomplishes His will and whose power rescues the perishing, and in Jesus that God has come near. He answers faith with saving power, even calling the dead back to life, a foretaste of His own resurrection and of the day He will raise all who are His and say, do not be afraid.

sermon.title: The Parable of the Sower
sermon.why: From Begg's series A Study in Luke, this message on Luke 8 opens the parable of the sower, showing how the same word meets different hearts and how only the good soil that receives and holds the word bears lasting fruit. A faithful, expository handling of the teaching that anchors this movement.

quiz.q: According to the parable of the sower, what determines whether the word bears fruit?

options.text: The condition of the heart that hears it, since the good soil hears, holds on, and perseveres.
options.note: Luke 8:15 makes the receptive, persevering heart the good soil that bears a crop.

options.text: How joyfully a person first receives the word.
options.note: The rocky soil received it with joy yet had no root; initial joy is not enough without perseverance.

options.text: The quality or strength of the seed being sown.
options.note: The seed, the word of God, is always good; the difference lies in the soil, not the seed.

options.text: Whether the sower scatters the seed skillfully.
options.note: The parable focuses on the soils, not the sower's technique.

quiz.q: Who did Jesus say His true mother and brothers are?

options.text: Those who hear the word of God and carry it out.
options.note: Luke 8:21 defines Jesus' family by hearing and doing God's word.

options.text: Those who listen carefully to the word of God.
options.note: Hearing is essential, but Jesus adds 'and carry it out'; doing completes it.

options.text: His biological mother and brothers who came to see Him.
options.note: They prompted the saying, but Jesus redefined family around obedience to the word.

options.text: The crowds who gathered to watch His miracles.
options.note: Mere spectators are not His family; hearing and doing the word is the mark.

quiz.q: What did the calming of the storm reveal about Jesus?

options.text: That He is Lord even over wind and water, doing what Scripture ascribes to God alone.
options.note: The disciples' question in Luke 8:25 recognizes an authority that belongs to God.

options.text: That He could protect His disciples from danger.
options.note: True, but the deeper revelation is His divine authority over creation itself.

options.text: That the storm happened to die down when He awoke.
options.note: It was no coincidence; Jesus rebuked the wind and waters and they obeyed.

options.text: That the disciples had nothing to fear from nature at all.
options.note: The point is not that nature is harmless but that Jesus commands it.

quiz.q: Why did Jesus ask 'Who touched Me?' in the crowd?

options.text: To draw the healed woman's hidden faith into the open and send her away in peace.
options.note: Luke 8:47-48 shows Jesus bringing her trembling faith to public confession and assurance.

options.text: To distinguish real faith from the crowd merely pressing against Him.
options.note: A true observation, but the fuller aim was to draw her out and give her peace, not only to make a distinction.

options.text: Because He did not actually know who had touched Him.
options.note: Jesus knew power had gone out from Him; He asked to draw the woman out, not from ignorance.

options.text: To rebuke the woman for touching Him while unclean.
options.note: He does not rebuke her; He calls her Daughter and commends her faith.

quiz.q: What did Jesus tell Jairus when news came that his daughter had died?

options.text: Do not be afraid; just believe, and she will be healed.
options.note: Luke 8:50 records Jesus' call to faith in the face of death.

options.text: It is too late now, but her death is not the end.
options.note: Death was indeed not the end, but Jesus' actual word was a summons to believe, not resignation.

options.text: That nothing more could be done for the girl.
options.note: The opposite; Jesus went on to raise her, calling Jairus to believe.

options.text: That Jairus should have brought Him sooner.
options.note: Jesus offers no reproach, only a call to trust Him.

questions: Jesus says the same word falls on every kind of heart. Which soil best describes how you have been receiving God's word lately, and what would it take to become good soil that holds on and perseveres?
The thorns are the worries, riches, and pleasures of life that choke the word. Which of these is most likely to crowd out God's word in your life right now?
In the storm Jesus asked, 'Where is your faith?' When fear rises, where do you tend to lose sight of who is in the boat with you?

The bleeding woman reached out in trembling, secret faith, and Jesus drew her into the open and called her Daughter. Is there a place where you need to bring your quiet faith, or your shame, honestly to Him?
To a grieving father Jesus said, do not be afraid, only believe. Where is Jesus calling you to trust Him in a situation that looks, humanly, already lost?

translationNotes.note: The words 'though seeing, they may not see' quote Isaiah 6:9; parables both reveal the kingdom to the receptive and veil it from the hard of heart.
Manuscripts vary between Gerasenes, Gadarenes, and Gergesenes for the region across the lake; the BSB follows Gerasenes, all naming the largely Gentile territory east of Galilee.
The word rendered 'healed you' is the same Greek verb elsewhere translated 'saved you'; Jesus' healing of the body points to the fuller salvation received by faith.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Luke 8:1-56. Headings, line breaks, footnote and poem markers were skipped when building verse text. One em dash (verse 8) was converted to a comma. No dash characters remain.
